text: Which of the following are legal entry point methods that can be run from the command line? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: public static final void main(String[] args)

explanation: The traditional entry point requires public static void main(String[] args). The final modifier is allowed. Without a return type (B, F) it does not compile. Instance methods (C) and private methods (A) cannot be the entry point.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, p. 37

explanation: The order must be: package declaration → import statements → class declaration. Z (package) must come before Y (import) must come before X (class). Z, Y, X is valid. Z, X is also valid (imports are optional). Y, X is invalid because there is a package statement that must come first if present.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, p. 22

text: Which statements about garbage collection in Java are true? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: System.gc() guarantees garbage collection runs immediately.
An object is eligible for GC when no strong references point to it.
Calling System.gc() is a hint, not a command.
Finalize() is guaranteed to run before an object is collected.
An object can become eligible for GC even if it is still referenced by another eligible object (island of isolation).

explanation: System.gc() is only a hint. finalize() is not guaranteed to run. Objects in a reference cycle with no external references form an island of isolation and are eligible for GC.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 72–74

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Java supports widening primitive conversions without a cast: int → long → double. x prints as 10 (int), y prints as 10 (long), z prints as 10.0 (double) because doubles always display with a decimal point.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 42–46

text: Which of the following is a valid use of the var keyword?
explanation: var requires an initializer so the compiler can infer the type. var x; and var x = null; do not provide enough information to infer a type, so they are compile errors. var cannot be used as a method return type. Nested var is not valid.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 64–69

text: Which of the following statements about wrapper classes are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: new Integer(42) is preferred over Integer.valueOf(42).
Autoboxing converts a primitive int to an Integer automatically.

explanation: parseInt returns a primitive; valueOf returns a wrapper object. new Integer(int) is deprecated since Java 9 — valueOf should be used (it may cache values). Autoboxing/unboxing are compiler-level automatic conversions. Integer.MAX_VALUE is 2^31 - 1 = 2,147,483,647.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 55–59

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Text blocks strip the common leading whitespace. The content is "Hello,\nWorld!\n" (with a trailing newline before the closing """). That is 6 + 1 + 6 + 1 = 14 characters.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 162–166

text: What is the result of the following code?

options.text: Compile error: possible lossy conversion from int to byte.
Assigns -56 to b due to overflow.

explanation: The expression b * 2 promotes b to int, resulting in an int. Assigning an int back to byte without a cast causes a compile-time error 'possible lossy conversion from int to byte'. b *= 2 would work because compound assignment operators include an implicit cast.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 2 – Operators, pp. 91–93

text: Which of the following are valid Java identifiers? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Java identifiers must start with a letter, underscore (_), or dollar sign ($), followed by letters, digits, underscores, or dollar signs. 3rdItem starts with a digit (invalid). class and true are reserved keywords/literals and cannot be used as identifiers.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 24–26

text: Which of the following correctly declares and initializes multiple variables in a single statement?
explanation: Multiple variables of the same type can be declared in one statement separated by commas, each with its own initializer (A). B is valid for declaration but x is not initialized. C mixes types in one declaration — invalid. var cannot declare multiple variables in the same statement (D).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 62–64

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: String literals are interned in the string pool, so a == b is true. new String("hello") creates a new object on the heap, so a == c is false (different references). equals() compares content, so a.equals(c) is true.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 157–160

text: Which primitive type can hold the value 4,000,000,000?
explanation: int holds up to ~2.1 billion (2^31 - 1). 4,000,000,000 exceeds int's range. long holds up to ~9.2 × 10^18, so it can store 4,000,000,000. float could also store this value approximately, but with loss of precision; long stores it exactly. The best answer for exact storage is long.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 40–44

text: Which of the following are true about the scope of local variables in Java? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: A local variable declared inside a for loop is not accessible after the loop ends.
Local variables are automatically initialized to their default values.
Two local variables with the same name can exist in nested blocks.
A local variable must be definitely assigned before it can be read.
Method parameters are a special kind of local variable.

explanation: Local variables are NOT automatically initialized (unlike instance/class variables). Reading an uninitialized local variable is a compile error. You cannot shadow a local variable with another local in a nested block (C is false). Loop variables are scoped to the loop. Method parameters count as local variables.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 60–62

text: What happens when the following code is compiled and run?

options.text: Compilation error: underscores are not allowed in numeric literals.

explanation: Java supports underscores in numeric literals for readability (1_000_000 = 1000000), hexadecimal literals (0xFF = 255), and binary literals (0b1010 = 10). All are stored as int and print in decimal.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 46–48

text: What is the output of the following code?

options.text: Compilation error: count and flag are not initialized.

explanation: Instance and static fields are automatically initialized to their default values: numeric types to 0, boolean to false, object references to null. Local variables are not, but instance variables are — so count = 0 and flag = false.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 1 – Building Blocks, pp. 58–60

text: What is the output of the following code snippet?

options.text: The code does not compile because one of the assignments requires an explicit numeric cast.

explanation: star = 2 + (2*3) = 8. 8 > 10 is false so sun = 3.0f. jupiter = (3.0 + 9) - 1.0 = 11.0. --moon = 8, 8 <= 8 is true so mars = 2.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 2 – Operators, pp. 83–98

text: What is the output of the following application?
explanation: ++x makes x=6, then 6*3=18, then --x makes x=5, 18/5=3 (integer division), 3+10=13. So x=5, y=13.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 2 – Operators, pp. 88–90

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: 2024 is a leap year, so February has 29 days. Adding one month to Jan 31 adjusts to the last valid day of February → Feb 29.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 180–183

text: What is the output of the following String method calls?
explanation: substring(7) returns from index 7 to end: 'World!'. substring(7, 12) returns from index 7 (inclusive) to 12 (exclusive): 'World' (W=7, o=8, r=9, l=10, d=11).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 157–162

text: What is the output of the following StringBuilder code?
explanation: After insert(5, " World"), sb is 'Hello World'. delete(0, 5) removes characters from index 0 (inclusive) to 5 (exclusive), removing 'Hello'. Result: ' World' (with leading space).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 167–172

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Math.min(3,7) returns int 3. Math.pow(2,10) returns double 1024.0. Math.abs(-5) returns int 5. System.out.println of a double prints with decimal point.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 191–194

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: ldt starts at hour 10. Adding a Duration of 5 hours gives hour 15. LocalDateTime supports Duration (which works with time units). LocalDate cannot use Duration.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 183–190

text: Which of the following statements about Period and Duration are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Period works with date-based values (years, months, days).
Duration works with time-based values (hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds).
Period can be added to a LocalTime.
Duration can be added to a LocalDateTime.
Both Period and Duration are immutable.

explanation: Period is date-only (years/months/days). Duration is time-based. Period cannot be added to LocalTime (no date component). Duration can be added to LocalDateTime. Both are immutable value objects.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 183–191

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Operator precedence: * and / before + and -. 3*2=6, 8/4=2. Then left to right: 5+6=11, 11-2=9.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 2 – Operators, pp. 83–86

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Strings are immutable. concat() returns a new String — it does not modify s. Since the return value is not stored, s still refers to 'Java'.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 157–162

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: i1 is 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z. i2 is one day later: 2024-01-02T00:00:00Z. i1.isBefore(i2) returns true because i1 is chronologically before i2.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 190–194

text: What is the output of the following code?
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 2 – Operators, pp. 93–97

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Ternary is right-associative: first checks x > 5 (true), then evaluates x > 8 ? "high" : "mid". Since x=10 > 8 is true, returns "high".
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 2 – Operators, pp. 97–99

text: Which of the following are true about StringBuilder vs String? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: StringBuilder is mutable; String is immutable.
StringBuilder is thread-safe; String is not.
StringBuilder.reverse() reverses the character sequence in place.
String concatenation with + always creates a new String object.
StringBuilder.append() returns the same StringBuilder instance.

explanation: StringBuilder is mutable. StringBuffer (not StringBuilder) is thread-safe. StringBuilder.reverse() modifies the builder in place and returns the same instance. String + concatenation inside loops creates many objects, but the compiler may optimize. append() returns 'this' enabling chaining.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 167–172

text: What does the following code print?
explanation: Period.between(d1, d2) computes the period in years, months, days. From Jan 1 to Dec 31 in the same year is 0 years, 11 months, and 30 days. getMonths() returns 11, getDays() returns 30.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 4 – Core APIs, pp. 183–190
